coredata.dc:title: Passive hybridization of photovoltaic cells with a lithium-ion battery cell: An experimental proof of concept
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Leible, Valentin
Bessler, Wolfgang G.
coredata.dc:description: Passive hybridization refers to a parallel connection of photovoltaic and battery cells on the direct current level without any active controllers or inverters. We present the first study of a lithium-ion battery cell connected in parallel to a string of four or five serially-connected photovoltaic cells. Experimental investigations were performed using a modified commercial photovoltaic module and a lithium titanate battery pouch cell, representing an overall 41.7 W-peak (photovoltaic)/36.8 W-hour (battery) passive hybrid system. Systematic and detailed monitoring of this system over periods of several days with different load scenarios was carried out. A scaled dynamic synthetic load representing a typical profile of a single-family house was successfully supplied with 100 % self-sufficiency over a period of two days. The system shows dynamic, fully passive self-regulation without maximum power point tracking and without battery management system. The feasibility of a photovoltaic/lithium-ion battery passive hybrid system could therefore be demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage
Photovoltaic battery system
Lithium-ion battery
Parallel connection
Passive hybridization

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Lithium and other Non- Aqueous Batteries: ENG/APP

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PV/battery passive hybridization on the cell level
Five-cell PV string, different load scenarios

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of passive hybridization of PV and LIB on the cell level. (a) Parallel connection of photovoltaic (PV) cells and a single lithium-ion battery (LIB) cell. The additional diode prevents battery discharge to the solar cell overnight. (b) Comparison of voltage characteristics of PV cell and LIB cell, where the battery characteristics pin the PV cell close to the maximum power point (MPP) while the open-circuit voltage of the PV cell keeps the LIB cell below its charge cut-off voltage.
Electrical setup of the experiment with four-cell PV strings. P: PV cell, D: diode, B: LIB cell, R: resistor, S: switch, V: voltage measurement.

Photographs of the modified PV module setup. The upper panel shows the front view (note the pyranometer at the top left). The lower panel shows a detail of the back view, where a total of six diodes were soldered to the interconnectors between single PV cells. Black cable holders were glued to the plastic backcover.

Passive PV/LIB hybrid with 4 serially-connected PV cells, no load: Operation over 10 days. Here and in the following figures, a time value of 0 h corresponds to midnight before the start of measurement.
Night-time behavior of the passive PV/LIB hybrid with 4 serially-connected PV cells during (a) the first and (b) the third night.
Passive PV/LIB hybrid with 4 serially-connected PV cells and 1 W load: Operation over 3 days.
Passive PV/LIB hybrid without load: Comparison between 4 and 5 serially-connected PV cells over 1 day-operation.
Passive PV/LIB hybrid with 5 serially-connected PV cells, no load: Operation over 10 days including overcharge situations.

Passive PV/LIB hybrid with 5 serially-connected PV cells: Comparison of three different load situations. Upper row: 1 W constant load; middle row: 3 W constant load; lower row: dynamic load representing a single-family house.
Characteristic voltages of the used PV and LIB cells according to their data sheets (PV at standard test conditions).
Comparison of operation characteristics of the systems with four-cell and five-cell PV strings.
Comparison of operation characteristics of the three load scenarios studied with the five-cell PV string system.

head.ce:title: Passive hybridization of photovoltaic cells with a lithium-ion battery cell: An experimental proof of concept

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy Systems Technology (INES), Offenburg University of Applied Sciences, Badstrasse 24, 77652, Offenburg, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Energy Systems Technology (INES), Offenburg University of Applied Sciences, Badstrasse 24, 77652, Offenburg, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Passive hybridization refers to a parallel connection of photovoltaic and battery cells on the direct current level without any active controllers or inverters. We present the first study of a lithium-ion battery cell connected in parallel to a string of four or five serially-connected photovoltaic cells. Experimental investigations were performed using a modified commercial photovoltaic module and a lithium titanate battery pouch cell, representing an overall 41.7 W-peak (photovoltaic)/36.8 W-hour (battery) passive hybrid system. Systematic and detailed monitoring of this system over periods of several days with different load scenarios was carried out. A scaled dynamic synthetic load representing a typical profile of a single-family house was successfully supplied with 100 % self-sufficiency over a period of two days. The system shows dynamic, fully passive self-regulation without maximum power point tracking and without battery management system. The feasibility of a photovoltaic/lithium-ion battery passive hybrid system could therefore be demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Photovoltaic (PV)/battery systems for residential power supply, also referred to as home storage systems, have exhibited a significant growth over the past years, associated with a strong decrease of prices [1,2]. These systems are based on either alternating current (AC) architecture (direct current/alternating current, DC/AC, conversion between PV and house grid, and another DC/AC conversion between battery and house grid), which provides flexibility in terms of modularity and extensibility; or DC architecture (DC/DC conversion between PV and battery, and DC/AC conversion to house grid), which provides higher conversion efficiency [3]. Both systems require power electronics and controllers, including inverters, maximum power point (MPP) trackers, and battery management system. These components add to system cost and complexity. As PV and battery represent a hybrid power generation system, we have used the term active hybridization for these standard architectures [4].

In the present contribution we demonstrate an alternative approach towards PV/battery coupling, which we refer to as passive hybridization, and which we have previously investigated in a modeling and simulation study [4]. The approach is based on the parallel connection of PV cells and battery cells without any intermediate (“active”) inverters or controllers – simply by connecting the respective positive and negative terminals of PV and battery. This setup can show a self-regulating operation behavior: upon battery charge, voltage increase drives the PV cell towards zero current and therefore prevents battery overcharge. Furthermore, if voltage levels are well chosen, the battery can pin the PV to its MPP. Power from the PV/battery couple can be used either on the DC level by parallel connection of a consumer, or on the AC level by parallel connection of an inverter. The principle reaches back to 1968 when first patents were filed [5,6]. It has been used mainly for lead-acid batteries, but has more recently been investigated in the context of lithium-ion batteries (LIB), too [7–9]. We have previously investigated this system using a dynamic system model where a 240 W photovoltaic module was coupled with a LIB consisting of nine serially-connected lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells [4]. We could show that passive hybridization allows to satisfy dynamic load demands and, when used for powering a household, can provide a similarly high level of self-sufficiency as standard systems with active hybridization.

The previous studies have investigated the coupling of a PV module with a LIB module, that is, passive hybridization on the module level. This has advantages of being based on readily available commercial components, including lithium-ion batteries with safety mechanisms supervising, for example, operation voltage ranges. In the present study, we investigate the coupling of PV cells with a LIB cell, that is, passive hybridization on the cell level. This allows more flexible matching of the voltage levels of PV and LIB cells due to the small increment of an individual PV cell voltage (ca. 0.6 V). By enabling to match the voltage of a single LIB cell, module-level battery management can become obsolete.

This paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, the approach for the cell-level passive hybridization is described. Details on the experimental setup are given in Section 3. Results are shown and discussed in Section 4. Finally, the paper is concluded in Section 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PV/battery passive hybridization on the cell level
ce:section.ce:para: The approach followed in the present investigation is shown in Fig. 1 . Panel (a) shows how a number of single PV cells (each having a typical MPP voltage of around 0.6 V) are connected in series to what we here refer to as PV string. The string is connected in parallel to a single LIB cell. As different LIB chemistries show different voltage characteristics [10], the number of PV cells in the string can be adjusted depending on the LIB chemistry. PV string and LIB cell are connected in parallel to a DC load, which can be a DC/AC converter providing electrical energy to an AC consumer.

Panel (b) shows schematic voltage-current curves of a PV string (left) and voltage-depth of discharge (DOD) curves of a battery cell (right). The PV curve depends nonlinearly on the solar irradiation, while the battery curve depends nonlinearly on the current. The voltage levels need to fulfill two requirements. (1) The PV open-circuit voltage (OCV) should be equal to or lower than the LIB charge-cutoff voltage. This ensures that the battery will not be overcharged, as the increasing battery voltage during charge gradually drives the PV cells to zero current. (2) The flat middle part of the LIB curve should be close to the MPP voltage. This allows for high PV power both during power consumption (when the load utilizes PV power) and during battery charge. The main degrees of freedom that can be used to fulfill these two requirements are the number of PV cells in the string and the LIB chemistry.

The voltage curves also show why a diode is needed in the circuit: When the PV voltage is lower than the battery voltage, either because of low solar irradiation and/or because of high battery state of charge (SOC), the battery would discharge into the solar cells. This can be avoided by inserting a diode that allows only battery charge, but not battery discharge via the PV string.
We have put the schematic setup shown in Fig. 1 into practice on a laboratory scale. The electrical setup is shown in Fig. 2 and will be described in the following.

For the PV string, we apply a modified commercial PV module (Solarworld Sunmodule SW 150 poly R6A, maximum power 150 W-peak (Wp), open-circuit voltage 22.5 V, MPP voltage 18.3 V, MPP current 8.27 A, short circuit current 8.81 A, all values given at standard test conditions, STC). The original module consists of 36 polycrystalline single PV cells with a size of 156 mm × 156 mm. The cells are serially-connected via three parallel interconnectors. We opened the back plastic housing of the module at the locations of the interconnectors between the cells and thus were able to separate out and individually contact a string of four (for some experiments five) serially-connected cells. At one side of the string, we soldered a total of three Schottky diodes (Panjit SBT1545LSS, 45 V, 15 A), one to each of the three interconnectors. In order to increase the available current and match it to the battery cell (see below), we used two such strings and connected them in parallel. Photographs of the modified PV module, mounted on the rooftop laboratory, are shown in Fig. 3 .

As battery cell we used a lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA)/lithium titanate (LTO) cell (Leclanché, nominal capacity 16 Ah, nominal voltage 2.3 V, voltage range 1.7–2.7 V). This cell was selected for the present study because it provides a very high lifetime and robustness, which seemed favorable for this test system, and because we have investigated and characterized these cells before in detail [11]. The real capacity of the LIB cell,
C batt
, was determined with a low-current cycle (Basytec XCTS) using a constant-current constant-voltage (CCCV) charge (0.1C to 2.7 V, C/100 cutoff) followed by a CCCV discharge (0.1C to 1.7 V, C/100 cutoff) at an ambient temperature (CTS climate chamber) of 20 °C. The capacity determined such was
C batt
= 20.2
Ah
, which is considerably more than the nominal capacity (16 A h). This value was used to calculate the SOC during the passive hybrid experiments by Coulomb counting according to
(1)
SOC =
100
%
C batt
∫ −
I batt
( t )
d t ,
calibrated to 0% for a fully-discharged cell.

The battery cycler (Basytec XCTS) was also used as DC load. In different experiments it was used to either apply a constant power (1 W or 3 W) or a synthetic load profile representing a single-family house over several days. The synthetic load profile was taken from Ref. [12] (obtained with a load profile generator [13]) where a battery system of 5 kWh was investigated, and downscaled to the present LIB energy (36.8 Wh).

The modified PV module was installed at the roof of the “C” building of Offenburg University of Applied Sciences (48° 27.49′ N, 7° 56.6’ E) with an orientation of 20° south east and an inclination of 25°. A pyranometer (Kipp & Zonen, CM11) was installed at the same orientation and inclination, allowing an independent monitoring of the solar irradiation. The LIB cell was operated within a climate chamber (CTS) set to a constant temperature of 20 °C. The climate chamber was located in a laboratory room, and long copper cables (20 m, 50 mm2 cross section) were required to connect PV and LIB cells.

For monitoring of the device, voltage was measured at multiple locations within the circuit (cf. Fig. 2). The current of PV, battery and load was measured via shunt resistors (Weigel, 1 mΩ). Temperatures were monitored on the battery surface and on the ambient outside (Type K thermocouples). All data were recorded with a logger (Graphtec GL 240, 10 channels) with a time resolution of 1 s.

The characteristic voltages of PV and LIB are compared in Table 1 . As discussed above, a requirement for passive hybridization is the match of maximum voltage. When using a four-cell PV string, the OCV (2.5 V) is below the LIB charge cut-off voltage (2.7 V) and therefore fulfills the requirement for self-regulation. However, the MPP voltage (2.0 V) is quite lower than the optimum LIB voltage (2.3 V), therefore inefficient operation away from the MPP can be expected. In order to improve the performance, a five-cell PV string could be used. The higher average voltage (2.5 V) is expected to be beneficial for battery charge. However, the maximum voltage (3.1 V) is significantly above the LIB cut-off voltage, which can induce overcharge of LIB. Still, both configurations (four-cell and five-cell PV string) were investigated.
In the following, various studies with increasing operational complexity are presented. They allow to analyze the behavior of the passive hybrid system in a systematic way.

The first study uses a four-cell PV string connected to the LIB cell, but does not include any load. This setup allows to investigate the charging behavior of the battery. Prior to the experiment, the LIB cell was fully discharged using a CCCV protocol (0.1C to 1.7 V, C/100 cutoff) and then connected to the PV strings. The passive hybrid system was then operated continuously for 10 days.

Results of this experiment are shown in Fig. 4 . Panel (a) shows global irradiation and outdoor temperature. The experiment was carried out during summer, as also indicated by the outdoor high temperatures (up to 38 °C). Irradiation and temperature show the expected day-night cycles, and irradiation shows a fast intra-day dynamics due to partial clouding.

Measured system voltages are shown in Panel (b). The PV string voltage is the average voltage measured over the two PV strings (V1 and V2 in Fig. 2), and the battery cell voltage was measured directly at the terminals (V4 in Fig. 2). The PV voltage shows a strong day/night dynamics (>2 V variation) and a fast intra-day dynamics (ca. 0.3 V variation). During the day, it varies mostly between 2.2 and 2.4 V, with an observed maximum of 2.45 V, which is somewhat lower than the OCV at standard test conditions (2.50 V, cf. Table 1). This difference is likely due to the high outdoor temperature. The LIB cell voltage does not show a strong dynamics. At first this seems counter-intuitive, as in the parallel connection, both PV and LIB cells should be at the identical voltage. The difference can be explained by the presence of the diode (which prevents the cell voltage from falling below the PV voltage during night or low irradiation and at the same time keeps the PV voltage at a small positive value at night) and by an ohmic drop over the long connection cables. During the 10 days, battery voltage continuously increases from 2.0 V to 2.15 V, meaning that the battery gets charged.

The measured current is shown in Fig. 4(c), both for the PV strings (
I PV
, measured via shunt resistance R1 in Fig. 2) and for the LIB cell (
I batt
, shunt resistance R3). Here and throughout, we define the signs with respect to balance in the parallel connection, that is,
(2)
I PV
+
I batt
+
I load
= 0 ,
with
I LIB
< 0
for battery charge,
I LIB
> 0
for discharge and
I PV
> 0
for PV power production. This sign convention will prove useful when a load is connected (see below). In the present experiment, without load, the PV and LIB cells exhibit the same, but inverse current: the battery is charged by the PV cells. Panel (d) shows the electric power, using the same sign convention, that is,
(3)
P PV
+
P batt
+
P load
= 0 .
the power shows qualitatively the same behavior as the current. In absolute values, currents peak daily between 0.5 and almost 2 A, and power between 1 and 4 W. These values are quite low compared to the specifications (PV MPP current 8 A, LIB nominal 1 C current 16 A).

Finally, the SOC of the LIB cell is shown in panel (e). It shows a step-like behavior, increasing during the daytime and staying constant overnight. The SOC increase slows down as the experiment progresses; while during the first day, a Δ SOC of almost 7% is reached, it is only ca. 1% for the last day, despite the fact the irradiation is high and the SOC, even at the end of the 10 days, does not reach values beyond 40%. Clearly, the PV voltage is not sufficient to drive the battery to higher SOC. This is consistent with the low currents observed. We conclude that, already at moderate LIB SOC (40%, 2.15 V), the PV string operates well above MPP (2.03 V). The additional voltage drops over the cables and the diodes drives the PV string towards OCV, therefore preventing additional battery charge.

It is interesting to more closely investigate the night-time behavior. The SOC during the first and third nights is shown in Fig. 5 . In the first night, SOC drops by ca. 0.05% over 11 h. In the third night, the SOC drop is ca. 0.002% and therefore considerably less. This can be explained by the temperature dependence of the leakage current of the diode. The first night is rather warm (26 … 22 °C), while the third night is significantly cooler (16 … 14 °C). The higher leakage current caused by the higher temperature is clearly detectable. Still, the SOC drops are only small, demonstrating the good performance of the diodes for the present purpose.

In the previous Section, the passive hybrid system was operated without load. Here we present results with a constant DC load of 1 W. The load was controlled such that it switched on when the LIB voltage increased above 2.0 V, and switched off when the LIB voltage decreased below 1.7 V. This prevented the load to go into a fast on/off pattern resulting from battery voltage relaxation after switching.

The experiment was carried out over three days, starting with a fully-discharged battery. Results are shown in Fig. 6 . Panel (a) shows global solar irradiation and outdoor temperature, which are, again, representative of three summer days. The measured voltages of PV strings and LIB cell are shown in Panel (b), here scaled between 1.6 V and 2.4 V in order to make the dynamics more visible. The data confirm that the LIB voltage is below the PV voltage during daytime due to the voltage drops over the diode and the connection cables. By evaluating voltages measured at different locations in the electrical circuit, we can further deconvolute the voltage drop between PV and battery. These results are presented in Panel (c). They show that the main contribution (around 200 mV) can be attributed to the threshold voltage of the diodes, while the cables show cause drop of mostly below 50 mV. It can be noted that the drop over the cables becomes negative in early evening, corresponding to the time when the load is satisfied by the battery only, as discussed in the following.

Most relevant from point of view of the functionality are the current, Panel (d), and the power, Panel (e), of the three branches (PV strings, LIB cell, DC load) of the parallel connection. The load is constant at
P load
= − 1 W
as long as the LIB voltage is > 1.7 V and at
P load
= 0
W
otherwise, corresponding to the boundary conditions applied to the system. At any point in time, the power balance
P PV
+
P batt
+
P load
is fulfilled, that is, the load power is satisfied by both, PV and LIB power. These two contributions, however, show a strong dynamics.
P PV
nonlinearly follows the irradiation profile.
P batt
adjusts itself: the battery charges
(
P batt
< 0
)
during the day, where PV produces more power than needed for the load; and the battery discharges
(
P batt
> 0
)
in the evening where there is no more PV production, until the LIB discharge cut-off voltage (1.7 V, cf. Panel a) is reached. Then the load switches off, both PV and battery show zero power, and the LIB voltage relaxes to an open-circuit voltage of ca. 1.95 V. The current shows qualitatively the same behavior as the power, always satisfying the charge balance
I PV
+
I batt
+
I load
= 0
. It should be emphasized that, in this passive hybrid system, the constant load demand is satisfied at any point in time (as long as the battery is not empty), despite the strong fluctuations of solar irradiation, without requiring any active controlling.

Fig. 6(f) shows the battery SOC. It shows a day-night cycling typical for PV-battery systems, with SOCs peaking at values around 10% in the afternoons, and decreasing to 0% during a few hours as soon as there is no more PV production. The observation that the SOC does not reach very high values is consistent with the observations of the 10-day charging experiment discussed in the previous section: the PV operates too far away from MPP to allow high charging currents.

While in the experiments presented above the general functionality of the passive PV/LIB hybrid could be demonstrated, battery charge was only slow and incomplete. This is due to a mismatch between the PV and LIB voltage characteristics. We have therefore changed the setup to extend the PV strings to five serially-connected cells instead of four cells. This increases the PV voltage by 25%.

Fig. 7 shows results of a single-day battery charging without load. The upper and lower rows show the behavior of a PV string with four and five cells, respectively, the former representing the second day of the experiment presented in Fig. 4. As expected, the PV voltage shows a significantly higher level for the five-cell systems, cf. Panels (a) und (d). As a result, the current flowing between PV string and LIB cell is significantly, around a factor of ten, higher, peaking at 5 A (Panel e), while the four-cell PV string provides currents well below 1 A (Panel b). Indeed, the PV cells now get to operate closer to their MPP, which would provide 16.6 A at standard test conditions. As consequence, the battery becomes charged significantly faster and the SOC gets higher. For the five-cell string (Panel f), the SOC increases from full discharge (0%) to full charge (100 %) during the day, while for the four-cell string (Panel c), it merely increases from 7% to 16%.

The results demonstrate a huge operational benefit when changing the setup from four to five PV cells. However, due to the higher PV string voltage, the OCV rises above the charge cut-off voltage (cf. Table 1), which may cause battery overcharge. We have therefore continued the charge experiment over a total of ten days. Fig. 8 shows the results. Panel (a) shows global irradiation and temperature, still representative of partially cloudy summer days. Panel (b) compares the PV and LIB voltages. The PV shows voltages over 3 V during day 5, which is the coolest day of this period (PV voltage is known increase with decreasing temperature [14]). As consequence, LIB voltage rises above 2.8 V, which is well above the charge cut-off voltage of 2.7 V given by the manufacturer. The cut-off voltage is included as dashed line in Fig. 8(b). Starting at day 2, the measured LIB voltage is temporally, after day 5 even permanently exceeding this value. The current is shown in Panel (c). The first day shows a high current out of range of the present scale (cf. Fig. 7: up to 5 A), and the subsequent days show further small charging currents. As consequence, the SOC, shown in Panel d), already close to 100 % after the first day, continues increasing. It exceeds 100 % during the second day and rises up to 104% after ten days. Note that the SOC is defined based on charge throughput (cf. Eq. (1)) and scaled to the battery cell capacity obtained prior to this experiment under well-defined laboratory conditions, in particular at a well-defined temperature and adhering to the cut-off voltages (cf. Section 3). An SOC above 100 % means that, during the ten-day experiment, more capacity was charged into the cell than it was possible under the well-defined laboratory conditions. Therefore, both voltage and SOC clearly show that, in this system, the LIB cell is overcharged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Five-cell PV string, different load scenarios
ce:section.ce:para: We have further studied the system with five-cell PV strings by applying different DC loads, (a) a constant 1 W load, (b) a constant 3 W load, and (c) a dynamic load profile representing a single-family house with peak load up to approx. 30 W. The results of these experiments are shown in Fig. 9 .

The upper row of Fig. 9 shows the study for a constant 1 W load, starting with a fully-discharged battery cell and running over three days. Panel (a) shows the voltage. Both PV and LIB show a day-night pattern, which, in case of the LIB, originates from the nightly discharge into the load. Additionally, during daytime, the PV voltage shows fluctuation in the range of around 0.1 V. These fluctuations are on the time scale of several minutes and are due to moving clouds partially covering the sun, similar as in Fig. 6(a). The power balance is shown in Panel (b). The 1 W load was applied as soon as LIB voltage reached 2 V and was continuously switched on over the full three days. The figure shows how the load is supplied by the sum of PV and battery, and how the battery is charged
(
P batt
< 0
)
upon surplus PV energy during the day. During the night, the battery is discharged
(
P batt
> 0
)
. The battery SOC is shown in panel (c). It shows pronounced day/night cycling, being charged during the days and discharged (almost linearly) during night. During the first day, the battery is charged from 0% to 96%, and the maximum SOC rises to 98% during the second and third days. During the night, the load drains the battery down to approx. 70% SOC. The system is able to supply the constant load despite the strong dynamics of the PV production, both during the partially cloudy days (dynamics in the 10 min range) and during the day/night dynamics.

Due to the good performance of this setup, we have repeated this experiment with a load of 3 W. These results are shown in the middle row of Fig. 9. They are qualitatively similar to the 1 W load situation. However, the battery is not able to supply the load over the complete night; it gets fully discharged during late night and the load has to be switched off for approx. 1.5 h before the PV panels supply power again in the morning. The battery cycles between 0% and approx. 80%.

Finally, we have applied a dynamic load to the system, representing the situation of a single-family house during 48 h. These results are shown in the lower row of Fig. 9. Panel (h) shows the load dynamics: during night, small and fluctuating consumers are present (e.g., a fridge); there are load peaks in the morning, the noon, and, particularly, in the evening. It is interesting to note that, in this situation, the system is subjected to significant dynamics from both the solar irradiation and the consumer load. The SOC, presented in Panel (i), shows that the battery, starting at 50% SOC, shows again a day-night cycling, but never runs empty. Therefore, in this experiment, the load is always fully satisfied by the coupled PV-LIB system.
It is worth noticing that, throughout these studies, the battery never got in into an overcharge situation.

The experimental data shown in the previous Sections clearly demonstrate the feasibility of a passively hybridized PV-LIB system. At the same time, we have shown that the number of serially-connected PV cells (affecting the relative voltage levels of PV and LIB) and the type of load have a strong influence on the system behavior. It is therefore worthwhile investigating some performance figures in more detail.

Table 2 shows a comparison of operation characteristics of systems with four-cell and five-cell PV-strings. As shown in the Table, the use of five-cell strings increases currents and allows for higher battery SOC. Yet, they also have higher available surface for capturing solar energy. We have therefore quantified the power efficiency
η P
of the solar cells, calculated as
(4)
η P
=
P PV
G ⋅ A
,
where, G is the solar irradiation (in W/m2) and A the surface area of the used PV cells (in m2). The maximum efficiency during the 10-day charge experiment was quantified to 2.26% and 8.86% for the four-cell and five-cell systems, respectively. These numbers indicate that the five-cell system operates closer to the MPP, but still does not reach the possible maximum (15% according to data sheet). We can further compare the behavior during the operation of the system with a constant 1 W load over 72 h. The energy consumed by the load,
(5)
E load
= ∫
P load
( t )
d t ,
is different for the two systems because the load cannot be satisfied throughout the complete duration in case of the four-cell system. This can be further quantified by defining the self-sufficiency as
(6)
SS =
∫
P load
( t )
d t
∫
P
load ,
requested
( t )
d t
,
which is only 64.2% for the four-cell system, which means that the PV-LIB hybrid is not able to supply the load during 35.8% of the duration of the experiment. It is furthermore interesting to quantify the energy efficiency
η E
over the full duration as
(7)
η E
=
∫
P PV
( t )
d t
∫ G
( t )
⋅ A d t

For the three-day experiment, these values are 1.12% and 2.23% for the four-cell and five-cell systems, respectively. The value of the four-cell system is quite lower because of its lower self-sufficiency.

A comparison of operation characteristics of the three different load scenarios investigated with the five-cell system is given in Table 3 . Here, the consumed energy adds to values between ca. 70 Wh and 200 Wh. The 1 W and the dynamic loads can be fully supplied (100 % SS), while the 3 W load cannot (93% SS). Most interesting are the values of the energy efficiency, which are in the 2–4% range.

It is worthwhile comparing the performance of the new setup with that of a standard, active hybrid system where PV and battery are coupled with inverters, and where the PV panel is operated with an MPP tracker. The energy efficiency of our passive hybrid, in the range of 2–4%, is significantly lower than the efficiency expected from a standard PV system, which is typically around 15% [3]. The reason for the low efficiency of the new system is as follows. In the passive hybrid system, potentially available PV power not needed for either battery charge or load supply is not used; instead, the system regulates itself away from the MPP in order to adjust PV power according to the need. In a standard, active hybrid system with inverters and MPP tracker, the PV cells are continuously operated at maximum power. The surplus energy not needed for battery charge or load supply is fed into the grid. This is not being done in the passive hybrid, resulting in low energy efficiencies despite high self-sufficiencies. Whether or not this is a disadvantage depends on the point of view. In case the grid feed is either not possible (e.g., in off-grid systems) or not attractive (e.g., due to low feed-in tariffs), it does not matter that the PV is not operated at MPP.

The data discussed here show that the five-cell PV string has a superior performance over the four-cell PV string. However, a significant disadvantage is the fact the battery cell is being overcharged as the battery voltage increases above charge cut-off voltage. It is known that overcharging a lithium-ion battery can lead to fast aging [15] or even safety issues like thermal runaway [16]. In order to investigate this issue, we have carried out a post-test capacity measurement with the same protocol than the pre-test investigation (cf. Section 3). The post-test capacity was 20.5 A h, as opposed to the pre-test capacity of 20.2 A h. The slight increase in capacity might be due to activation effects, as for example caused by an anode overhang [17], or might simply be within the measurement uncertainty. In any case, we can conclude that the experiments carried out during this work, which include overcharging to 2.83 V and 104% SOC, did not harm the investigated cell.

We have presented an experimental study of a photovoltaic-battery system with passive hybridization. This means that photovoltaic (PV) cells, a lithium-ion battery (LIB) cell, and an electrical consumer were connected in parallel without using inverters or controllers. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic investigation of a LIB cell in parallel connection to a string of PV cells. The ability of the system to provide continuous (day and night) power to a consumer could be demonstrated. The system shows several self-regulating features, in particular a self-distribution of power between PV and LIB, and a cut-off of battery charge when the battery is full.

In the study we have compared setups with four-cell and five-cell PV-strings, which were realized by modifying a commercial PV module. This resulted in 33.3 Wp/36.8 Wh and 41.7 Wp/36.8 Wh PV/LIB systems, respectively. The five-cell system shows significantly better power performance, but leads to LIB overcharge in case of extended charging periods. We have also compared different load scenarios with either constant 1 W or 3 W load, and a dynamic load representing a single-family house. The PV/LIB passive hybrid is able to satisfy the load consumption despite strong dynamics in both solar irradiation and the load profile. High self-sufficiencies of up to 100 % over a three-day period could be demonstrated with the five-cell PV string.

The passive hybrid system has several advantages. There is no active control required for either the PV cells (no maximum power point, MPP, tracker) or the LIB (no battery management system). In case of alternating current (AC) loads, only a single direct current/alternating current (DC/AC) inverter is required. The simple architecture can make the system more robust and cheaper compared to standard architectures. The main disadvantage of the passive hybrid, compared to standard systems with inverter, is the low solar-to-electricity conversion efficiency because the PV module is generally not operated at the MPP and surplus solar energy is not fed into the grid. It will therefore be particularly suited for off-grid applications.
Valentin Leible: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Wolfgang G. Bessler: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
